concerns.text: The plan proposes fitting an attentional Drift Diffusion Model (aDDM) via grid search on 50,000 individual scenarios. aDDM fitting is computationally intensive (often requiring numerical integration of stochastic differential equations per trial). Performing this on 50k rows with 11 grid points per parameter on a 2-CPU runner within 30 minutes is methodologically unsound; the likelihood of non-convergence or timeout is near 100%, invalidating the statistical power of the study.

The plan conflates 'visual salience' (image-based) with 'text salience' (word-frequency) into a single predictor variable. These are distinct constructs with different psychometric properties. Combining them into one `salience_score` without a theoretical justification or separate modeling path violates construct validity, as the model cannot distinguish whether effects are driven by visual prominence or textual priming.

The sensitivity analysis sweeps decision thresholds over {0.01, 0.05, 0.10}. In aDDM literature, decision thresholds (boundary separation) are typically in the range of 0.5 to 2.0 (in evidence units). Values like 0.01 are effectively zero, implying immediate random guessing. This parameter sweep is methodologically invalid and will not test the robustness of the model in any meaningful regime.

The plan relies on Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) to handle collinearity between salience and attributes (e.g., humans are more salient than cats). VIF only flags the issue; it does not resolve it. If VIF > 5.0, the plan states it will 'flag' but continue. This leaves the core causal inference (salience effect) confounded and uninterpretable, failing to answer the research question.

The text-salience heuristic (word frequency/position) is a novel operationalization with no established validity for 'attentional drift' in moral dilemmas. Without a pilot study or validation against eye-tracking data (which is absent), using this as a primary predictor for aDDM drift rates lacks construct validity.
